title: Lesson 226
transcript: We just spent a bunch of time talking about all the different types of malware that are out there. Now let's talk about anti-malware systems. These are systems that are designed to try and prevent malware from getting onto our systems, or if it has gotten onto our systems, detect it and hopefully remove it. As we talked about related to malware, one of the most common types of malware is a virus. And as you may recall, the way that a virus is triggered is by a user doing something, clicking on something. And so one of the best ways of preventing malware is actually not any fancy technical systems. It's actually user awareness training to make the users aware that there's this thing called malware out there, there's viruses, and that they need to be a little bit careful about what they do with their systems and what they click on. In fact, one of the most common ways of breaking into a system nowadays is through phishing attacks, which is entirely reliant upon users clicking on something that they shouldn't. So one of the best ways of preventing malware is actually through policies, through training, through awareness to make users aware that they're not supposed to click on stuff. Now let's look at some of the systems that we can actually use to detect malware. The first systems we'll talk about are what are known as signature-based systems. Signature-based systems actually use a signature to detect malware. So what's a signature? While all the big anti-malware vendors out there, they're constantly monitoring the deep dark web for sketchy new pieces of malware that are out there. And every time that they detect a new piece of malware, they write a new signature, a way of fingerprinting or uniquely identifying that piece of malware. And then they constantly push out those signatures. And we all have to update our signature based system so that they can detect the latest, greatest types of malware. And signature-based systems are by far the most common types of systems to detect malware. But they have a really big Achilles heel, and that is, can a signature-based system ever detect a zero day? And the answer, of course, is no, because zero-day implies that it's the very first time that this piece of malware has ever been seen, and signature-based systems can only detect what they have a signature for, what they know about. And so there's no way that these systems can detect zero-day on the first day it's released. And that's actually where heuristic-based systems come in. Heuristic-based systems don't look for a particular signature. Instead, what they look for is they look at what the malware does. They look at what a file does and they try to determine, is this in fact a piece of malware? There's two major methods that a heuristic based system can try and detect a piece of malware, static and dynamic. So static means that the heuristic based system is actually scanning through the source code or scanning through the compiled code of the malware to try and determine, is it doing something malicious? Is it making certain function calls, changes to the registry, things like that. So that's static. The other method that a heuristic system can use is dynamic. And what that means is that the heuristic system actually runs the code runs the executable in a sandbox. And a sandbox, as you recall, is a protected, safe environment. And so the heostric-based system will run suspected malware in a sandbox and then watch what the malware does. Well, that's not as effective as you think it might be because most malware nowadays, most malware writers, they're well aware of heuristic based systems and especially sandboxes. And so they'll write their malware to check, am I running in a sandbox? And if the malware determines it's running in a sandbox, what do you think it does? It acts completely innocent. hi, I'm the nicest, innocent, most wonderful piece of software ever. And it just basically lies and it doesn't do any of the malicious things that it would normally do. And then as soon as it detects that it's no longer in a sandbox, ah, it jumps out and it attacks your system. So heuristic based systems, great idea, very difficult to implement. They're very susceptible to false positives and they don't necessarily catch everything. There's one other set of systems that we can use to detect malware. The first is what are known as activity monitors. Now, an activity monitor looks at the applications that are actually running, the running processes on your system. so this is really the uh the line of last resort because if an application if a piece of malware has gotten to the point where it's actually running on your system i mean it's installed at this point right so activity monitors are very much the uh the last resort but they can be useful so an activity monitor looks to see if a running process might be a piece of malware And then the final type is what are known as change detection systems. And as the name implies, they look for changes. Changes to what? Usually certain key system files. So this is commonly used on Linux-based systems, for instance. And a change detection system will look at certain key system files and will look to see if those files have been changed. Because that might be an indication that there's malware on the system, changing the system. How do they detect if there's been a change? Well, when you first turn them on, they will, and point them at a particular file that you want them to monitor, the system will hash the file. And then a few hours later, a day later, whatever, the change-based system will rehash the file and... compare the hash values. If they're the same, no changes. If the hash files don't match, if the hash values don't match, then you know there's been a change. A great example of this is Tripwire, very commonly used on Linux-based systems. Now, as I mentioned, the most common type of anti-malware system are signature-based systems. And one incredibly important thing that we have to constantly do with signature-based systems is Update them. You always continuously have to be updating the latest, greatest signatures or you're never going to be able to detect the latest malware.
